snippet7.summary:
Gets the lowest possible positive square root of 3 numbers (That's based on reading the method signature)

Reading through the code:
Calculates 'det' and does a sanity check/error case
Calculates a few other variables
If r1 is greater than r2, switch them around
Return whatever is positive checking in order r1 and r2
snippet7.comprehensibility:
I'm not familiar enough with the math to know the abbreviation 'det' or 'r'

I'm not familiar enough with it to know why we need to switch around r1 and r2

I would simplify it and combine the if statements, like if r1 is bigger just check if r2>0 and return it. the swapping of numbers seems unnecessary.

Using variable 'tmp' to switch things around is hard to keep track of at first glance, until you see it's just swapping numbers.

snippet2.summary: Checks if a URI is a remote(non-local) location
snippet2.comprehensibility:
Not clear why the hardcoded checks for 'file' and 'jar' are there. Maybe they cannot be remote?

Switch statement is easy to understand but turning it into an if statement would turn like 8 lines of code into 2

Or really I'd combine them all, ```if scheme == null || scheme.equals("file") || scheme.equals("jar")``` return false

I think when you have very limited cases, and when you're not really doing different things with those cases (in this case, we have 3 cases that do 1 thing, return false) the switch statement is overkill

snippet4.summary:
Based on method signature - indexOf method but it ignores case, with optional start position parameter (assuming it's optional based on other indexOf implementations I've seen)

Scanning code:
error case check up front
default case check for start pos
Set array index bounds
error case check that string is 1 char or less
error case that search string is empty (I don't think returning start pos makes sense here I would return index not found but as long as that's documented that could be OK)

Now scan through string and see if each substring is the string we're looking for
snippet4.comprehensibility:
I found it flowed very naturally and was easy to understand. The variable names made a lot of sense and each if statement had a clear check it was doing.

My only question was line 13-14 why return startPos. Maybe that's standard practice and I haven't used IndexOf recently enough.

This is probably how I would code it up making it as clear as possible (like for an interview)

Most straightforward code so far but maybe biased because it's a simple index of check

snippet1.summary:
validation method for a string name

Makes sure the name isn't empty, doesn't start with a period, isn't too long

Makes sure each char of string is a letter or digit and is within some Constant set of allowed characters
snippet1.comprehensibility:
Isn't clear why we need both the letter or digit check and also valid name set. I would think the valid name set could easily contain all letters and numbers and avoid the other check. Because of the AND in the if statement, things need to be in the valid name set, so I would drop that separate check for isLetterOrDigit

Otherwise very easy to understand and follow

snippet6.summary: Get encoded length of a string (as Char Sequence)
snippet6.comprehensibility:
This code confuses me but I'm also not very familiar with UTF 8 vs. 16 and the bitwise stuff that's going on.

line 7 while loop confuses me. It's probably important but I don't know why. Not sure what the <0x80 does. But it looks important. This will increment 'i' to ~something~ for the next loop

For loop - more comparisons to binary stuff that doesn't mean anything to me. Would have to look up these special characters.

In the end it seems to be incrementing the utf8Length based on the string somehow, doing a error case check, and returning it. Overall not very comprehensible to me. Anytime you're doing stuff like this, you NEED comments. the one comment 'Optimized Implementation' just tells me the obvious, or tells me someone did this on purpose at least.

snippet8.summary:
A complete guess is that this is related to arctangent but I could be wrong, not familiar with 'atan' otherwise. I would guess it takes the square of arc tangent? Don't know enough about math to know why XD

Reading the rest of the code, it returns 'atanUnchecked' so maybe it's literally like 'V2' of atan
snippet8.comprehensibility:
using x y and n for variables makes it not so easy to comprehend

not clear what the if/else is doing line 4-7. I can understand it's setting some initial values in edge cases but not sure why

The code itself is straightforward, the if statements are simple and things like method calls and reference to PI makes sense but I don't know enough to say if it's correct or why it's doing these things

not clear what Fields we're checkign or what 'isSynthetic' is doing

If it's not synthetic, check the modifiers and see if they're static and final, and then if they are and the field matches our type then it's valid? that's my quick read.

Incomprehensible because I would need context of what is a Machine Type, what is CoffMachineType class. What does isSynthetic do?
And why would I check all the fields of that class?

Would need to see the class to know. I would think we would define some array of types and use that

snippet5.summary: It deletes all files and directories inside a given filesystem path.
snippet5.comprehensibility: The comprehensibility is good except for the name of the function. The main function `deleteRecursively` being named `-recursively` may lead a code reader into thinking that the method was implemented using recursion. In this case the method itself is not written recursively (Files.walkFileTree may or may not use a recursive pattern but this is out of the scope of this function). Instead the method could be called "deepDelete" or something similar to avoid reading the method looking for a recursive call that doesn't actually happen.

snippet8.summary: I'm not sure what this snippet does. I guess it's trying to compute the atan2 mathematical function but I it's definitely not implemented properly.
snippet8.comprehensibility:
The code is terrible. The first if/else condition does nothing as the if conditions for lines 4 and 6 are never true.

The last line (line 19) returns the sum of two coordinates, when the atan2 function should return a result in degrees or radians.

Lines 15 and 17 are operating between a coordinate value and a radians value, which again doesn't make sense.

snippet6.summary:
What it does: Depending on the input string the code throws an index out of bounds exception in line 12.

What maybe it tries to do: It may try to encode the integer value corresponding to the length of a characters sequence into the integer representation of a the length of a string in UTF-8. This description doesn't even make sense but it may be that someone needs that at some point (?)
snippet6.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is extremely bad. The factors that make it hard to understand are:

- UTF-8 is a string encoding. From line 1 you would expect the length of the input CharSequence to be encoded as a UTF-8 string, but instead the method returns an integer.
- line 7 gives a condition to a loop that depends both on the length of the input CharSequence as well as the specific values contained in this sequence. Not clear why ascii values above 0x80 would not be considered.
- Checking the 0x80 value (128) we can assume that most characters given in a CharSequence will be under 128, hence line 12 will in most cases throw an index out of bounds exception.
- Line 14 uses bit shift which can be clever and optimised for some operations but normally doesn't lead to easy understanding of code in most scenarios.
- Line 16 calls another function that probably does something very similar to the current function but it's unclear what we could expect from a function that uses an unreliable index and the input sequence as parameters.
- In general the function is extremely cryptic and most likely incorrect

snippet3.summary: In case the CoffMachineType has declared fields the code throws a null pointer exception in line 11. Otherwise it will return false
snippet3.comprehensibility:
The code is difficult to comprehend. The following reasons explain this:

- By the name it's expected that the short representation of a type (input value) is checked to know whether it is defined as a "MachineType".
- Line 2 validation is very specific and covers only the case when the type is associated to an `IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE` and this is unknown. This is strange as you would expect to either having a wide range of unkown types (not only images), or a validation that is not so specific as to know what kind of unknown type is given.
- Line 6 explicitly checks values for a CoffMachineType. By the definition of the main function you would expect the function to cover more cases than a specific CoffMachineType, but it could be that this is the only machine ever defined in the system.
- Line 11 will always throw a null pointer exception trying to convert null into a Short

Nevertheless I think the variable naming could be improved. In addition to it using Float is not the best decision give it could lead to arithmetic mistakes. Instead something like BigDecimal could be a better choice.

Original:

The comprehensibility is bad.

- The main name makes you understand that we'll be trying to find the lowest root for the three input numbers, but the math inside the method is quite confusing.
- Line 2 computes what by the name of the variable would be expected to be a "determinant", making you think that the values come from a matrix, but this doesn't make sense.
- Line 6 would make you think an inverse of a number is being calculated, but it's rather the "double multiplicative inverse".
- The code does some sorting in an ascending manner but it's not clear whether the values that are being sort make sense. It could be that all the math executed in lines 2 to 8 are indeed some "root" computation for 3 input float numbers. In that case the code would make some sense.

Given that we don't know the implementation of the `regionMatches` method we have to assume that the second or fourth parameters indicate that case-sensitivity has to be ignored in the matching.

phase3.comparison:2-3:2|5: While reading the `deleteRecursively` method for the first time I was looking for the recursion pattern in the code and instead the code used the Files.walkFileTree method to traverse the files. phase3.comparison:3-4:5|4: `deleteRecursively` is still clearer than `indexOfIgnoreCase` in that the method reads quite nicely with well defined variable names and usage of the familiar Files java library. Even though `indexOfIgnoreCase` is a simple method I still consider that its implementation needs a bit more thinking and mental compilation than the `deleteRecursively` method.
phase3.comparison:4-5:4|7: `indexOfIgnoreCase` requires less background knowledge to understand what the method is doing. snippet6.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is extremely bad. The factors that make it hard to understand are:

- UTF-8 is a string encoding. From line 1 you would expect the length of the input CharSequence to be encoded as a UTF-8 string, but instead the method returns an integer.
- line 7 gives a condition to a loop that depends both on the length of the input CharSequence as well as the specific values contained in this sequence. Not clear why ascii values above 0x80 would not be considered.
- Checking the 0x80 value (128) we can assume that most characters given in a CharSequence will be under 128, hence line 12 will in most cases throw an index out of bounds exception.
- Line 14 uses bit shift which can be clever and optimised for some operations but normally doesn't lead to easy understanding of code in most scenarios.
- Line 16 calls another function that probably does something very similar to the current function but it's unclear what we could expect from a function that uses an unreliable index and the input sequence as parameters.
- In general the function is extremely cryptic and most likely incorrect

snippet3.comprehensibility:
The code is difficult to comprehend. The following reasons explain this:

- By the name it's expected that the short representation of a type (input value) is checked to know whether it is defined as a "MachineType".
- Line 2 validation is very specific and covers only the case when the type is associated to an `IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE` and this is unknown. This is strange as you would expect to either having a wide range of unkown types (not only images), or a validation that is not so specific as to know what kind of unknown type is given.
- Line 6 explicitly checks values for a CoffMachineType. By the definition of the main function you would expect the function to cover more cases than a specific CoffMachineType, but it could be that this is the only machine ever defined in the system.
- Line 11 will always throw a null pointer exception trying to convert null into a Short

On the other hand the valid project name function is very self explanatory.
phase3.comparison:2-3:2|5: While reading the `deleteRecursively` method for the first time I was looking for the recursion pattern in the code and instead the code used the Files.walkFileTree method to traverse the files. Because of this reading this code was a bit more confusing than the snippet checking whether a URI is remote.
phase3.comparison:3-4:5|4: `deleteRecursively` is still clearer than `indexOfIgnoreCase` in that the method reads quite nicely with well defined variable names and usage of the familiar Files java library. Despite the fact that the method uses a Visitor pattern, even not knowing what the pattern exactly does a reader could understand the file tree traversing method happening based on the names of the overridden methods for the SimpleFileVisitor instance. Even though `indexOfIgnoreCase` is a simple method I still consider that its implementation needs a bit more thinking and mental compilation than the `deleteRecursively` method.
phase3.comparison:4-5:4|7: `indexOfIgnoreCase` requires less background knowledge to understand what the method is doing. The issue with `lowestPositiveRoot` is still that the names are not fully explicit and the fact that we're using the quadratic formula is not given anywhere in the code. Some comments or better naming could make this more explicit.
phase3.comparison:5-6:7|3: The difference in the ranking for these two methods is a bit harder to give as they sit close in how comprehensible they are. I still consider that `lowestPositiveRoot` reads easier than `isMachineTypeDefined` once you know you're dealing with the quadratic formula. On the other hand `isMachineTypeDefined` has the difficulties of dealing with the reflection libraries which is always a bit confusing.
phase3.comparison:6-8:3|8,6: `isMachineTypeDefined` is still a well defined method that can be understood even if it has some struggles due to the reflection library used. On the other hand both `encodedLength` and `atan2` methods are still very cryptic on their implementations. Despite other experts clarifications I still find the explanations not fully convincing and the two methods feel equally incomprehensible to me.
phase3.grouping:8:8,6: After reading the feedback from other experts, it's still unclear to me whether the methods actually do what they are intended to do. One of the experts explained that `atan2` is an approximation method but I'm still not convinced the mathematics behind it are sound. And `encodedLenght` using binary shifts and validating a set of ascii characters, that to me still don't make sense, make this method not easy to comprehend. I still think they are equally cryptic and possibly equally flawed

Edit: Initially I didn't notice that the code was using the quadratic formula to compute the roots of a second degree polynomial. I'm changing this review after I realised this is the case after the final questions in the survey.

Original response: 

Not sure what this code does. Depending on the inputs it could return a Float.NaN. Otherwise it does some seemingly random computations and returns one of those computations as a result.
snippet7.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is alright. Given that I didn't learn this formula in English I'm not sure if the components of the equation are called something similar to Det and Inv. Nevertheless I think the variable naming could be improved. In addition to it using Float is not the best decision give it could lead to arithmetic mistakes. Instead something like BigDecimal could be a better choice.

---
Original:

The comprehensibility is bad.

- The main name makes you understand that we'll be trying to find the lowest root for the three input numbers, but the math inside the method is quite confusing.
- Line 2 computes what by the name of the variable would be expected to be a "determinant", making you think that the values come from a matrix, but this doesn't make sense.
- Line 6 would make you think an inverse of a number is being calculated, but it's rather the "double multiplicative inverse".
- The code does some sorting in an ascending manner but it's not clear whether the values that are being sort make sense. It could be that all the math executed in lines 2 to 8 are indeed some "root" computation for 3 input float numbers. In that case the code would make some sense.

snippet4.summary: The code returns the index of a substring `searchStr` inside a given string `str` using an offset of `startPos` to search inside the `str`. Allegedly the function ignores case sensitivity. In cases when the `searchStr` is an empty string the `startPos` value is returned.
snippet4.comprehensibility:
The snippet is ok. The good things:
- Uses well defined constants to express the concept of an `INDEX_NOT_FOUND`
- Variables have meaningful names
- Defines variables with useful concepts that build upon each other to compute the business logic.

Given that we don't know the implementation of the `regionMatches` method we have to assume that the second or fourth parameters indicate that case-sensitivity has to be ignored in the matching.

phase3.comparison:1-2:1|2: Because the problem of defining a valid project name is more straightforward than that of defining a URI as local. The URI class is one of those classes that you don't use everyday and you need to go to check the documentation to remember how it is composed. On the other hand the valid project name function is very self explanatory.
phase3.comparison:2-3:2|5: While reading the `deleteRecursively` method for the first time I was looking for the recursion pattern in the code and instead the code used the Files.walkFileTree method to traverse the files. Because of this reading this code was a bit more confusing than the snippet checking whether a URI is remote.
phase3.comparison:3-4:5|4: `deleteRecursively` is still clearer than `indexOfIgnoreCase` in that the method reads quite nicely with well defined variable names and usage of the familiar Files java library. Despite the fact that the method uses a Visitor pattern, even not knowing what the pattern exactly does a reader could understand the file tree traversing method happening based on the names of the overridden methods for the SimpleFileVisitor instance. Even though `indexOfIgnoreCase` is a simple method I still consider that its implementation needs a bit more thinking and mental compilation than the `deleteRecursively` method.
phase3.comparison:4-5:4|7: `indexOfIgnoreCase` requires less background knowledge to understand what the method is doing. The issue with `lowestPositiveRoot` is still that the names are not fully explicit and the fact that we're using the quadratic formula is not given anywhere in the code. Some comments or better naming could make this more explicit.
phase3.comparison:5-6:7|3: The difference in the ranking for these two methods is a bit harder to give as they sit close in how comprehensible they are. I still consider that `lowestPositiveRoot` reads easier than `isMachineTypeDefined` once you know you're dealing with the quadratic formula. On the other hand `isMachineTypeDefined` has the difficulties of dealing with the reflection libraries which is always a bit confusing.
phase3.comparison:6-8:3|8,6: `isMachineTypeDefined` is still a well defined method that can be understood even if it has some struggles due to the reflection library used. On the other hand both `encodedLength` and `atan2` methods are still very cryptic on their implementations. Despite other experts clarifications I still find the explanations not fully convincing and the two methods feel equally incomprehensible to me.
phase3.grouping:8:8,6: After reading the feedback from other experts, it's still unclear to me whether the methods actually do what they are intended to do. One of the experts explained that `atan2` is an approximation method but I'm still not convinced the mathematics behind it are sound. And `encodedLenght` using binary shifts and validating a set of ascii characters, that to me still don't make sense, make this method not easy to comprehend. I still think they are equally cryptic and possibly equally flawed

snippet2.summary: The snippet defines any URI that points to a file system path, a file, a jar, or lacks a URI scheme definition, as non-remote URIs.
snippet2.comprehensibility: The comprehensibility is not bad but it's not perfect either. Part of the check is given by a call to a function to assess if the URI points to a file system path, and the rest of the code makes a remoteness decision based on the scheme of the URI. As these are two independent grouping categories, the code could be clearer making the separation of concerns a bit more explicit (for instance having an `isSchemeRemote`/`isSchemeLocal` function. Nevertheless the code is short enough that it is still comprehensible.

snippet5.summary: It deletes all files and directories inside a given filesystem path.
snippet5.comprehensibility: The comprehensibility is good except for the name of the function. The main function `deleteRecursively` being named `-recursively` may lead a code reader into thinking that the method was implemented using recursion. In this case the method itself is not written recursively (Files.walkFileTree may or may not use a recursive pattern but this is out of the scope of this function). Instead the method could be called "deepDelete" or something similar to avoid reading the method looking for a recursive call that doesn't actually happen.

snippet8.summary: I'm not sure what this snippet does. I guess it's trying to compute the atan2 mathematical function but I it's definitely not implemented properly.
snippet8.comprehensibility:
The code is terrible. The first if/else condition does nothing as the if conditions for lines 4 and 6 are never true.

The last line (line 19) returns the sum of two coordinates, when the atan2 function should return a result in degrees or radians.

Lines 15 and 17 are operating between a coordinate value and a radians value, which again doesn't make sense.

In order to analise the rest of the code I'd need to review what the atan2 function math is, but I think it's not necessary as the rest of the implementation is so flawed.

snippet6.summary:
What it does: Depending on the input string the code throws an index out of bounds exception in line 12.

What maybe it tries to do: It may try to encode the integer value corresponding to the length of a characters sequence into the integer representation of a the length of a string in UTF-8. This description doesn't even make sense but it may be that someone needs that at some point (?)
snippet6.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is extremely bad. The factors that make it hard to understand are:

- UTF-8 is a string encoding. From line 1 you would expect the length of the input CharSequence to be encoded as a UTF-8 string, but instead the method returns an integer.
- line 7 gives a condition to a loop that depends both on the length of the input CharSequence as well as the specific values contained in this sequence. Not clear why ascii values above 0x80 would not be considered.
- Checking the 0x80 value (128) we can assume that most characters given in a CharSequence will be under 128, hence line 12 will in most cases throw an index out of bounds exception.
- Line 14 uses bit shift which can be clever and optimised for some operations but normally doesn't lead to easy understanding of code in most scenarios.
- Line 16 calls another function that probably does something very similar to the current function but it's unclear what we could expect from a function that uses an unreliable index and the input sequence as parameters.
- In general the function is extremely cryptic and most likely incorrect

snippet3.summary: In case the CoffMachineType has declared fields the code throws a null pointer exception in line 11. Otherwise it will return false
snippet3.comprehensibility:
The code is difficult to comprehend. The following reasons explain this:

- By the name it's expected that the short representation of a type (input value) is checked to know whether it is defined as a "MachineType".
- Line 2 validation is very specific and covers only the case when the type is associated to an `IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE` and this is unknown. This is strange as you would expect to either having a wide range of unkown types (not only images), or a validation that is not so specific as to know what kind of unknown type is given.
- Line 6 explicitly checks values for a CoffMachineType. By the definition of the main function you would expect the function to cover more cases than a specific CoffMachineType, but it could be that this is the only machine ever defined in the system.
- Line 11 will always throw a null pointer exception trying to convert null into a Short

snippet1.summary: The code validates whether the input name is valid, given the restrictions defined in the application (nullability, length, and valid characters).
snippet1.comprehensibility:
The code is easy to comprehend.

The code is well structured, as each condition evaluates an independent factor to know whether the input string is a valid project name.

The evaluated conditions also build upon the previous ones making the code easy to follow when reading it sequentially.

phase3.comparison:3-4:5|4: `deleteRecursively` is still clearer than `indexOfIgnoreCase` in that the method reads quite nicely with well defined variable names and usage of the familiar Files java library. Despite the fact that the method uses a Visitor pattern, even not knowing what the pattern exactly does a reader could understand the file tree traversing method happening based on the names of the overridden methods for the SimpleFileVisitor instance. Even though `indexOfIgnoreCase` is a simple method I still consider that its implementation needs a bit more thinking and mental compilation than the `deleteRecursively` method.
phase3.comparison:4-5:4|7: `indexOfIgnoreCase` requires less background knowledge to understand what the method is doing. The issue with `lowestPositiveRoot` is still that the names are not fully explicit and the fact that we're using the quadratic formula is not given anywhere in the code. Some comments or better naming could make this more explicit.
phase3.comparison:5-6:7|3: The difference in the ranking for these two methods is a bit harder to give as they sit close in how comprehensible they are. I still consider that `lowestPositiveRoot` reads easier than `isMachineTypeDefined` once you know you're dealing with the quadratic formula. On the other hand `isMachineTypeDefined` has the difficulties of dealing with the reflection libraries which is always a bit confusing.
phase3.comparison:6-8:3|8,6: `isMachineTypeDefined` is still a well defined method that can be understood even if it has some struggles due to the reflection library used. On the other hand both `encodedLength` and `atan2` methods are still very cryptic on their implementations. Despite other experts clarifications I still find the explanations not fully convincing and the two methods feel equally incomprehensible to me.
phase3.grouping:8:8,6: After reading the feedback from other experts, it's still unclear to me whether the methods actually do what they are intended to do. One of the experts explained that `atan2` is an approximation method but I'm still not convinced the mathematics behind it are sound. And `encodedLenght` using binary shifts and validating a set of ascii characters, that to me still don't make sense, make this method not easy to comprehend. I still think they are equally cryptic and possibly equally flawed

snippet2.summary: The snippet defines any URI that points to a file system path, a file, a jar, or lacks a URI scheme definition, as non-remote URIs.
snippet2.comprehensibility: The comprehensibility is not bad but it's not perfect either. Part of the check is given by a call to a function to assess if the URI points to a file system path, and the rest of the code makes a remoteness decision based on the scheme of the URI. As these are two independent grouping categories, the code could be clearer making the separation of concerns a bit more explicit (for instance having an `isSchemeRemote`/`isSchemeLocal` function. Nevertheless the code is short enough that it is still comprehensible.

snippet5.summary: It deletes all files and directories inside a given filesystem path.
snippet5.comprehensibility: The comprehensibility is good except for the name of the function. The main function `deleteRecursively` being named `-recursively` may lead a code reader into thinking that the method was implemented using recursion. In this case the method itself is not written recursively (Files.walkFileTree may or may not use a recursive pattern but this is out of the scope of this function). Instead the method could be called "deepDelete" or something similar to avoid reading the method looking for a recursive call that doesn't actually happen.

snippet8.summary: I'm not sure what this snippet does. I guess it's trying to compute the atan2 mathematical function but I it's definitely not implemented properly.
snippet8.comprehensibility:
The code is terrible. The first if/else condition does nothing as the if conditions for lines 4 and 6 are never true.

The last line (line 19) returns the sum of two coordinates, when the atan2 function should return a result in degrees or radians.

Lines 15 and 17 are operating between a coordinate value and a radians value, which again doesn't make sense.

In order to analise the rest of the code I'd need to review what the atan2 function math is, but I think it's not necessary as the rest of the implementation is so flawed.

snippet6.summary:
What it does: Depending on the input string the code throws an index out of bounds exception in line 12.

What maybe it tries to do: It may try to encode the integer value corresponding to the length of a characters sequence into the integer representation of a the length of a string in UTF-8. This description doesn't even make sense but it may be that someone needs that at some point (?)
snippet6.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is extremely bad. The factors that make it hard to understand are:

- UTF-8 is a string encoding. From line 1 you would expect the length of the input CharSequence to be encoded as a UTF-8 string, but instead the method returns an integer.
- line 7 gives a condition to a loop that depends both on the length of the input CharSequence as well as the specific values contained in this sequence. Not clear why ascii values above 0x80 would not be considered.
- Checking the 0x80 value (128) we can assume that most characters given in a CharSequence will be under 128, hence line 12 will in most cases throw an index out of bounds exception.
- Line 14 uses bit shift which can be clever and optimised for some operations but normally doesn't lead to easy understanding of code in most scenarios.
- Line 16 calls another function that probably does something very similar to the current function but it's unclear what we could expect from a function that uses an unreliable index and the input sequence as parameters.
- In general the function is extremely cryptic and most likely incorrect

snippet3.summary: In case the CoffMachineType has declared fields the code throws a null pointer exception in line 11. Otherwise it will return false
snippet3.comprehensibility:
The code is difficult to comprehend. The following reasons explain this:

- By the name it's expected that the short representation of a type (input value) is checked to know whether it is defined as a "MachineType".
- Line 2 validation is very specific and covers only the case when the type is associated to an `IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE` and this is unknown. This is strange as you would expect to either having a wide range of unkown types (not only images), or a validation that is not so specific as to know what kind of unknown type is given.
- Line 6 explicitly checks values for a CoffMachineType. By the definition of the main function you would expect the function to cover more cases than a specific CoffMachineType, but it could be that this is the only machine ever defined in the system.
- Line 11 will always throw a null pointer exception trying to convert null into a Short

snippet1.summary: The code validates whether the input name is valid, given the restrictions defined in the application (nullability, length, and valid characters).
snippet1.comprehensibility:
The code is easy to comprehend.

The code is well structured, as each condition evaluates an independent factor to know whether the input string is a valid project name.

The evaluated conditions also build upon the previous ones making the code easy to follow when reading it sequentially.

snippet7.summary:
This code gives the root for a second degree polynomial of the form ax^2+bx+c=0.

Edit: Initially I didn't notice that the code was using the quadratic formula to compute the roots of a second degree polynomial. I'm changing this review after I realised this is the case after the final questions in the survey.

---
Original response: 

Not sure what this code does. Depending on the inputs it could return a Float.NaN. Otherwise it does some seemingly random computations and returns one of those computations as a result.
snippet7.comprehensibility:
The comprehensibility is alright. Given that I didn't learn this formula in English I'm not sure if the components of the equation are called something similar to Det and Inv. Nevertheless I think the variable naming could be improved. In addition to it using Float is not the best decision give it could lead to arithmetic mistakes. Instead something like BigDecimal could be a better choice.

---
Original:

The comprehensibility is bad.

- The main name makes you understand that we'll be trying to find the lowest root for the three input numbers, but the math inside the method is quite confusing.
- Line 2 computes what by the name of the variable would be expected to be a "determinant", making you think that the values come from a matrix, but this doesn't make sense.
- Line 6 would make you think an inverse of a number is being calculated, but it's rather the "double multiplicative inverse".
- The code does some sorting in an ascending manner but it's not clear whether the values that are being sort make sense. It could be that all the math executed in lines 2 to 8 are indeed some "root" computation for 3 input float numbers. In that case the code would make some sense.

snippet4.summary: The code returns the index of a substring `searchStr` inside a given string `str` using an offset of `startPos` to search inside the `str`. Allegedly the function ignores case sensitivity. In cases when the `searchStr` is an empty string the `startPos` value is returned.
snippet4.comprehensibility:
The snippet is ok. The good things:
- Uses well defined constants to express the concept of an `INDEX_NOT_FOUND`
- Variables have meaningful names
- Defines variables with useful concepts that build upon each other to compute the business logic.

Given that we don't know the implementation of the `regionMatches` method we have to assume that the second or fourth parameters indicate that case-sensitivity has to be ignored in the matching.

snippet5.summary: This method deletes the file or directory at the given path passed into the method. If the path is a directory, it recursively deletes all files and subdirectories, then deletes the directory itself. It returns true only if the path is non-null and exists.
snippet5.comprehensibility:
I assessed the snippet’s comprehensibility by reading it top-down and checking for clear and traditional structure, familiar APIs, generally accepted naming conventions, and consistent flow. The method is relatively easy to understand because it uses a straightforward control flow and well-known Java NIO constructs like Files.walkFileTree and SimpleFileVisitor. The overridden visitFile and postVisitDirectory methods make the deletion logic explicit and predictable.

The main difficulty is that understanding recursive deletion through a file-tree visitor requires familiarity with how walkFileTree behaves, so someone new to that API might find it less intuitive. Additionally, the snippet provides no comments explaining why these methods are overridden or what assumptions are being made about edge cases (e.g., symbolic links, partial failures). Brief comments could make the intent clearer and help readers understand the boundaries of its correctness.

snippet3.summary: This method checks whether the given type value corresponds to any of the machine type constants defined in the CoffMachineType class. It skips the IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE_UNKNOWN value, then uses reflection to loop through the class’s static final fields and compares each one’s short value to the input. If it finds a match, it returns true; otherwise it returns false
snippet3.comprehensibility: I read through the snippet from top to bottom and focused on what the method was trying to check and how it went about doing that. The basic idea is pretty easy to follow once you notice it’s looping over constants in a class, but the use of reflection makes it a little harder to read at first glance. Reflection typically requires a moment to think about what fields are being inspected and why. The check for static final fields helps clarify the intent, but it’s still not immediately obvious unless you’re familiar with how reflection works in Java. A short comment explaining why reflection is being used would make the code a bit easier to digest. Overall, it’s understandable, just not as straightforward as it would be without reflection.

snippet6.summary: This method appears to be calculating how many bytes a given CharSequence would take if it were encoded as UTF-8.  The method iterates through the characters, skipping over the ASCII range, then adjusts the count for multi-byte UTF-8 characters. If the resulting length would overflow an int data type, it throws an exception. Otherwise, it returns the computed UTF-8 byte length.
snippet6.comprehensibility: I read through the snippet step by step and tried to figure out how the length calculation worked and why certain branches existed. I don't think overall idea is too hard to follow once you realize it’s computing UTF-8 length from UTF-16 characters, and the early loop that skips ASCII makes sense. The harder part is the bit-level expressions like ((0x7f - c) >>> 31), which aren’t immediately obvious unless you’ve seen or worked with that style of optimization before. The lack of comments around the more complex arithmetic also makes it take a little longer to understand and discern what’s going on. Overall, it’s readable, but some of the low-level operations require a little extra thinking.

snippet2.summary: This method checks whether a given URI should be considered “remote.” It returns false: (1) for filesystem paths; (2) if the URI has no scheme; or (3) if the scheme is something local like file or jar. For anything else, it treats the URI as remote and returns true.
snippet2.comprehensibility: I went through the snippet line by line and looked at the early-return conditions to see how it decides whether a URI is considered remote. The logic is pretty straightforward, and the switch statement makes the handling of local schemes clear. The only part that requires a little guesswork is the call to isFilesystemPath(uri) since that method isn’t shown— a short comment explaining what counts as a filesystem path would make that intention a bit clearer. Otherwise, the code is easy to read and generally understand.

snippet7.summary: This method solves a quadratic equation and returns the smallest positive root. It first checks the discriminant, then computes both roots, sorts them so the smaller one is ordered first, and returns the first positive one it finds. If neither root is positive or the discriminant is negative, it returns Float.NaN.
snippet7.comprehensibility: I started by looking at the parameters and then read through the method from top to bottom to see how it handles the quadratic equation. The flow is easy to follow because it more or less mirrors how you’d solve a quadratic by hand: check the discriminant, compute both roots, sort them, and then pick the smallest positive one. The variable names also follow normal mathematical naming conventions, which makes it easier to keep track of what’s going on. The only part that takes a second to think about is the little swap section to reorder the roots, but even that makes sense once you notice why it’s there. Overall, the method is clear and pretty straightforward to understand.

snippet1.summary: This method checks whether a given project name is valid. It rejects names that are: (1) null, (2) start with a dot, (3) are too short (less than 1) or too long (over a defined maximum length), or (4) contain characters that aren’t letters, digits, or part of an allowed predefined character set. If the name passes all those checks, it returns true.
snippet1.comprehensibility: I started by looking at the method signature and then read through the methods and its various checks from top to bottom. The code is pretty easy to understand because each condition is simple and the early returns make the logic straightforward. The variable and method names also make the intent clear — it’s obvious this is validating a project name. The only part that requires a quick glance is the reference to the predefined character set, since you’d need to see what’s in that set to know exactly what’s allowed. A comment may be useful there. Other than that, the snippet is clean and very readable.

snippet8.summary: This method computes an approximation of atan2(y, x), which is the angle of the point (x, y) in radians. It handles different quadrants by checking the signs of x and y, uses a fallback when the y/x ratio is undefined or produces NaN, and then adjusts the result with either pi or pi/2 depending on where the point lies. The actual angle calculation is done through a helper method called atanUnchecked.
snippet8.comprehensibility: I looked at the parameters first and then read through the method from top to bottom to see how it was trying to approximate atan2. The general structure appears to be similar to how you’d reason about the problem by hand — compute the y/x ratio and then figure out the correct quadrant based on the signs of x and y. That part makes the intent fairly easy to follow. The harder part is the NaN handling using expressions like n != n and n - n != n - n, which isn’t very intuitive unless you’ve seen that pattern before. It’s also not a traditional way to check for NaN, since most code would use built-in helper functions like Float.isNaN(), so it takes a moment to understand what the code is doing. Without comments explaining those cases, you have to stop and think about what’s going on. Overall, the snippet is understandable, but the NaN checks make it harder to read than it should be.

snippet4.summary: This method performs a case-insensitive search for a substring within a larger character sequence, starting at a given position passed in as a parameter to the function. If it finds a match, it returns the index where the match begins. If the input is invalid or no match is found, it returns a special “not found” value.
snippet4.comprehensibility: I started by looking at the parameters and then read through the method from top to bottom to see how it handled the search. The overall flow is pretty easy to follow because the conditions are simple and the early returns make the logic clear. The variable names are straightforward and descriptive, and the loop that checks each position lines up with how you’d expect a substring search to work. The only part that isn’t immediately obvious is what regionMatches does without seeing its implementation, but the name gives you a good hint, and a short comment there might help if that method isn't in the same file or nearby. Overall, the snippet is clean and easy to understand.

phase3.comparison:1-2:1|2:
I found the project name validation snippet easier to understand than the URI one because everything it does is right there in front of you and self-contained. It just walks through a series of simple checks on a single string: null, leading dot, length range, and allowed characters. The loop is straightforward, the conditions are easy to read, and the intent matches the method name exactly. 

The isRemote method is still pretty readable, but it pulls in a bit more context. You have to reason about URI schemes and what counts as “remote”.  And, there’s also a dependency on isFilesystemPath(uri), whose behavior you don’t see in this snippet and must infer. That extra bit of indirection and the need to think and infer about external behavior makes it slightly less immediate to understand than the project name validator.
phase3.comparison:2-3:2|4:
The isRemote snippet is easier to understand because it’s shorter, more linear, and deals with only a couple of simple conditions. You can read straight through it and immediately see the intent: check a few properties of the URI and return true or false based on the scheme. There are no loops and the branching stays relatively shallow.  

The indexOfIgnoreCase snippet, while still readable, requires a little more mental work to parse out its logic. It has more parameters to consider, several edge-case checks, and a loop that searches through the characters. snippet5.summary: This method deletes the file or directory at the given path passed into the method. If the path is a directory, it recursively deletes all files and subdirectories, then deletes the directory itself. It returns true only if the path is non-null and exists.
snippet5.comprehensibility:
I assessed the snippet’s comprehensibility by reading it top-down and checking for clear and traditional structure, familiar APIs, generally accepted naming conventions, and consistent flow. The method is relatively easy to understand because it uses a straightforward control flow and well-known Java NIO constructs like Files.walkFileTree and SimpleFileVisitor. The overridden visitFile and postVisitDirectory methods make the deletion logic explicit and predictable.

The main difficulty is that understanding recursive deletion through a file-tree visitor requires familiarity with how walkFileTree behaves, so someone new to that API might find it less intuitive. Additionally, the snippet provides no comments explaining why these methods are overridden or what assumptions are being made about edge cases (e.g., symbolic links, partial failures). Brief comments could make the intent clearer and help readers understand the boundaries of its correctness.

snippet3.summary: This method checks whether the given type value corresponds to any of the machine type constants defined in the CoffMachineType class. If it finds a match, it returns true; otherwise it returns false

snippet7.summary: This method solves a quadratic equation and returns the smallest positive root. The loop is straightforward, the conditions are easy to read, and the intent matches the method name exactly. 

The isRemote method is still pretty readable, but it pulls in a bit more context. You have to reason about URI schemes and what counts as “remote”.  And, there’s also a dependency on isFilesystemPath(uri), whose behavior you don’t see in this snippet and must infer. That extra bit of indirection and the need to think and infer about external behavior makes it slightly less immediate to understand than the project name validator.
phase3.comparison:2-3:2|4:
The isRemote snippet is easier to understand because it’s shorter, more linear, and deals with only a couple of simple conditions. You can read straight through it and immediately see the intent: check a few properties of the URI and return true or false based on the scheme. There are no loops and the branching stays relatively shallow.  

The indexOfIgnoreCase snippet, while still readable, requires a little more mental work to parse out its logic. It has more parameters to consider, several edge-case checks, and a loop that searches through the characters. You also have to reason about the starting position, the calculated end boundary, and infer what regionMatches does. None of the code is overly complex, but there are simply more moving parts than in isRemote, which makes it take a bit more effort to follow.
phase3.comparison:3-4:4|5:
The substring search method is easier to understand because everything it’s doing is contained within the method itself. Even though it has a loop and a few boundary checks, the overall idea is straightforward: adjust the starting position, calculate how far you can search, and then scan through the string looking for a match. The control flow is familiar and the logic stays localized. 

Conversely, the recursive delete method requires understanding how Files.walkFileTree works and how the SimpleFileVisitor callbacks interact with it. The actual deletion logic is simple, but the pattern itself isn’t something you interpret linearly—you have to mentally connect the visitor methods to the traversal process and understand that the framework is controlling the recursion. Because the behavior depends on the external file-tree traversal API rather than just the code in front of you, it takes more effort to follow and understand what’s happening in the function.
phase3.comparison:4-5:5|7:
The recursive delete snippet is easier to understand because the overall intent is clear right away: walk through a directory tree and delete everything in it. Even though it uses walkFileTree, the logic inside the visitor is simple—delete each file, then delete each directory. Once you recognize the pattern, the flow is pretty easy to follow.

The quadratic-root snippet requires more mental work because you have to track the math. You need to remember how the discriminant works, how the quadratic formula is applied, and why the roots are swapped. The code itself isn’t really complicated — it’s the underlying math that makes you pause. A brief comment explaining the steps of the quadratic calculation would make it easier to understand more quickly. The method is still readable, but you do have to think through the algebra and the ordering logic, which makes it a bit more involved than just iterating over a file tree and deleting entries.
phase3.comparison:5-6:7|3:
The quadratic-root snippet, even though it requires some math, has a very linear and predictable flow. You compute the discriminant, calculate two roots, reorder them if necessary, and then return the smallest positive one. The logic is contained entirely within the method, and once you understand the math, the code itself is straightforward and easy to follow, particularly with the variables named appropriately.

The machine-type snippet is harder because it relies heavily on reflection, which is much less intuitive to read. Instead of simply working with local variables, the code dynamically inspects fields on another class, checks their modifiers, and retrieves values at runtime. Understanding what’s happening means keeping track of the reflection API, the meaning of synthetic fields, and why static final fields are being filtered. Without comments, you also have to infer why reflection is even being used. All of that requires more mental overhead and analysis outside of the function itself than following the quadratic formula.
phase3.comparison:6-7:3|6:
The machine-type snippet is easier to understand because, even though it uses reflection, the overall idea is straightforward: loop through a set of constants and check whether any of them match the input value. Once you recognize the pattern, the flow is pretty direct, and most of the complexity comes from the API calls rather than any tricky or confusing logic.  

The UTF-8 encoded-length snippet is more difficult because the logic is dense and optimized in ways that aren’t immediately obvious or intuitive. You have to understand character ranges, multi-byte UTF-8 encoding rules, and why certain bit-level expressions, like ((0x7f - c) >>> 31), work the way they do. There’s also an abrupt jump to a helper method when a non-ASCII, non-2-byte character is found, which adds another layer you have to understand and think about. The method is correct but compact and “clever,” so it takes more effort to unpack than the reflection loop.
phase3.comparison:7-8:6|8:
The UTF-8 length calculation snippet is tricky because of the bit-level optimizations, but the overall structure is still pretty logical. It walks through the characters, handles ASCII first, then moves on to multi-byte ranges, and finally checks for overflow. Even if you don’t fully understand the condensed/clever arithmetic, you can still follow the high-level idea.

The atan2 snippet is harder because the flow is much less intuitive. It uses unusual NaN-detection tricks like n != n and n - n != n - n, which aren’t patterns most developers use or encounter, since they would typically rely on built-in helper functions. On top of that, the method branches into several quadrant-handling cases, and the fallback returns involve expressions like x + HALF_PI, which require you to reason about what x actually represents at that point. Without comments, you have to mentally reconstruct how each case produces the correct angle. All of that makes the atan2 code more confusing and less straightforward than the UTF-8 length calculation.

snippet1.summary:
Gets the lowest possible positive square root of 3 numbers (That's based on reading the method signature)

Reading through the code:
Calculates 'det' and does a sanity check/error case
Calculates a few other variables
If r1 is greater than r2, switch them around
Return whatever is positive checking in order r1 and r2
snippet1.comprehensibility:
I'm not familiar enough with the math to know the abbreviation 'det' or 'r'

I'm not familiar enough with it to know why we need to switch around r1 and r2

I would simplify it and combine the if statements, like if r1 is bigger just check if r2>0 and return it. the swapping of numbers seems unnecessary.

Using variable 'tmp' to switch things around is hard to keep track of at first glance, until you see it's just swapping numbers.

snippet2.summary: Checks if a URI is a remote(non-local) location
snippet2.comprehensibility:
Not clear why the hardcoded checks for 'file' and 'jar' are there. Maybe they cannot be remote?

Switch statement is easy to understand but turning it into an if statement would turn like 8 lines of code into 2

Or really I'd combine them all, ```if scheme == null || scheme.equals("file") || scheme.equals("jar")``` return false

I think when you have very limited cases, and when you're not really doing different things with those cases (in this case, we have 3 cases that do 1 thing, return false) the switch statement is overkill

snippet3.summary:
Based on method signature - indexOf method but it ignores case, with optional start position parameter (assuming it's optional based on other indexOf implementations I've seen)

Scanning code:
error case check up front
default case check for start pos
Set array index bounds
error case check that string is 1 char or less
error case that search string is empty (I don't think returning start pos makes sense here I would return index not found but as long as that's documented that could be OK)

Now scan through string and see if each substring is the string we're looking for
snippet3.comprehensibility:
I found it flowed very naturally and was easy to understand. The variable names made a lot of sense and each if statement had a clear check it was doing.

My only question was line 13-14 why return startPos. Maybe that's standard practice and I haven't used IndexOf recently enough.

This is probably how I would code it up making it as clear as possible (like for an interview)

Most straightforward code so far but maybe biased because it's a simple index of check

snippet4.summary:
validation method for a string name

Makes sure the name isn't empty, doesn't start with a period, isn't too long

Makes sure each char of string is a letter or digit and is within some Constant set of allowed characters
snippet4.comprehensibility:
Isn't clear why we need both the letter or digit check and also valid name set. I would think the valid name set could easily contain all letters and numbers and avoid the other check. Because of the AND in the if statement, things need to be in the valid name set, so I would drop that separate check for isLetterOrDigit

Otherwise very easy to understand and follow

snippet5.summary: Get encoded length of a string (as Char Sequence)
snippet5.comprehensibility:
This code confuses me but I'm also not very familiar with UTF 8 vs. 16 and the bitwise stuff that's going on.

line 7 while loop confuses me. It's probably important but I don't know why. Not sure what the <0x80 does. But it looks important. This will increment 'i' to ~something~ for the next loop

For loop - more comparisons to binary stuff that doesn't mean anything to me. Would have to look up these special characters.

In the end it seems to be incrementing the utf8Length based on the string somehow, doing a error case check, and returning it. Overall not very comprehensible to me. Anytime you're doing stuff like this, you NEED comments. the one comment 'Optimized Implementation' just tells me the obvious, or tells me someone did this on purpose at least.

A complete guess is that this is related to arctangent but I could be wrong, not familiar with 'atan' otherwise. I would guess it takes the square of arc tangent? Don't know enough about math to know why XD

Reading the rest of the code, it returns 'atanUnchecked' so maybe it's literally like 'V2' of atan
snippet6.comprehensibility:
using x y and n for variables makes it not so easy to comprehend

not clear what the if/else is doing line 4-7. I can understand it's setting some initial values in edge cases but not sure why

The code itself is straightforward, the if statements are simple and things like method calls and reference to PI makes sense but I don't know enough to say if it's correct or why it's doing these things

This code confuses me but I'm also not very familiar with UTF 8 vs. 16 and the bitwise stuff that's going on.

line 7 while loop confuses me. It's probably important but I don't know why. Not sure what the <0x80 does. But it looks important. This will increment 'i' to ~something~ for the next loop

For loop - more comparisons to binary stuff that doesn't mean anything to me. Would have to look up these special characters.

In the end it seems to be incrementing the utf8Length based on the string somehow, doing a error case check, and returning it. Overall not very comprehensible to me. Anytime you're doing stuff like this, you NEED comments. the one comment 'Optimized Implementation' just tells me the obvious, or tells me someone did this on purpose at least.

snippet1.summary:
Gets the lowest possible positive square root of 3 numbers (That's based on reading the method signature)

Reading through the code:
Calculates 'det' and does a sanity check/error case
Calculates a few other variables
If r1 is greater than r2, switch them around
Return whatever is positive checking in order r1 and r2
snippet1.comprehensibility:
I'm not familiar enough with the math to know the abbreviation 'det' or 'r'

I'm not familiar enough with it to know why we need to switch around r1 and r2

I would simplify it and combine the if statements, like if r1 is bigger just check if r2>0 and return it. the swapping of numbers seems unnecessary.

Using variable 'tmp' to switch things around is hard to keep track of at first glance, until you see it's just swapping numbers.

snippet2.summary: Checks if a URI is a remote(non-local) location
snippet2.comprehensibility:
Not clear why the hardcoded checks for 'file' and 'jar' are there. Maybe they cannot be remote?

Switch statement is easy to understand but turning it into an if statement would turn like 8 lines of code into 2

Or really I'd combine them all, ```if scheme == null || scheme.equals("file") || scheme.equals("jar")``` return false

I think when you have very limited cases, and when you're not really doing different things with those cases (in this case, we have 3 cases that do 1 thing, return false) the switch statement is overkill

snippet3.summary:
Based on method signature - indexOf method but it ignores case, with optional start position parameter (assuming it's optional based on other indexOf implementations I've seen)

Scanning code:
error case check up front
default case check for start pos
Set array index bounds
error case check that string is 1 char or less
error case that search string is empty (I don't think returning start pos makes sense here I would return index not found but as long as that's documented that could be OK)

Now scan through string and see if each substring is the string we're looking for
snippet3.comprehensibility:
I found it flowed very naturally and was easy to understand. The variable names made a lot of sense and each if statement had a clear check it was doing.

My only question was line 13-14 why return startPos. Maybe that's standard practice and I haven't used IndexOf recently enough.

This is probably how I would code it up making it as clear as possible (like for an interview)

Most straightforward code so far but maybe biased because it's a simple index of check

snippet4.summary:
validation method for a string name

Makes sure the name isn't empty, doesn't start with a period, isn't too long

Makes sure each char of string is a letter or digit and is within some Constant set of allowed characters
snippet4.comprehensibility:
Isn't clear why we need both the letter or digit check and also valid name set. I would think the valid name set could easily contain all letters and numbers and avoid the other check. Because of the AND in the if statement, things need to be in the valid name set, so I would drop that separate check for isLetterOrDigit

Otherwise very easy to understand and follow

Using variable 'tmp' to switch things around is hard to keep track of at first glance, until you see it's just swapping numbers.

snippet2.summary: Checks if a URI is a remote(non-local) location
snippet2.comprehensibility:
Not clear why the hardcoded checks for 'file' and 'jar' are there. Maybe they cannot be remote?

Switch statement is easy to understand but turning it into an if statement would turn like 8 lines of code into 2

Or really I'd combine them all, ```if scheme == null || scheme.equals("file") || scheme.equals("jar")``` return false

I think when you have very limited cases, and when you're not really doing different things with those cases (in this case, we have 3 cases that do 1 thing, return false) the switch statement is overkill

snippet3.summary:
Based on method signature - indexOf method but it ignores case, with optional start position parameter (assuming it's optional based on other indexOf implementations I've seen)

Scanning code:
error case check up front
default case check for start pos
Set array index bounds
error case check that string is 1 char or less
error case that search string is empty (I don't think returning start pos makes sense here I would return index not found but as long as that's documented that could be OK)

Now scan through string and see if each substring is the string we're looking for
snippet3.comprehensibility:
I found it flowed very naturally and was easy to understand. The variable names made a lot of sense and each if statement had a clear check it was doing.

This is probably how I would code it up making it as clear as possible (like for an interview)

snippet4.summary:
validation method for a string name

Makes sure the name isn't empty, doesn't start with a period, isn't too long

Makes sure each char of string is a letter or digit and is within some Constant set of allowed characters
snippet4.comprehensibility:
Isn't clear why we need both the letter or digit check and also valid name set. I would think the valid name set could easily contain all letters and numbers and avoid the other check. Because of the AND in the if statement, things need to be in the valid name set, so I would drop that separate check for isLetterOrDigit

Otherwise very easy to understand and follow

snippet5.summary: Get encoded length of a string (as Char Sequence)

This code confuses me but I'm also not very familiar with UTF 8 vs. 16 and the bitwise stuff that's going on.

line 7 while loop confuses me. It's probably important but I don't know why. Not sure what the <0x80 does. But it looks important. This will increment 'i' to ~something~ for the next loop

For loop - more comparisons to binary stuff that doesn't mean anything to me. Would have to look up these special characters.

In the end it seems to be incrementing the utf8Length based on the string somehow, doing a error case check, and returning it. Overall not very comprehensible to me. snippet1.summary: This snippet searches the fields of the `CoffMachineType` class for one whose `getShort` method returns a value equal to the input short. If any such value is found, the snippet returns true; otherwise, it returns false. It also returns false if the input matches a constant (`IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE_UNKNOWN`). These shorts seem to represent something specific, but it's not clear what from the snippet itself.
snippet1.comprehensibility: The use of Java's reflective features to read the class literal of `CoffMachineType` is relatively complex, and makes it a bit hard to understand the purpose of the method. I also don't really understand what `type` is actually supposed to represent - it seems to be a fake enumeration of some kind, but the actual specification can't be discerned from what we can see. So, I think this snippet is fairly difficult to understand.

snippet2.summary:
This is a string validation method implementing the follow rules:
* the input must be nonnull
* the input must not start with a dot
* the input must not be the empty string
* the input must be shorter than or equal to the `MAX_NAME_LENGTH` constant
* each character of the string must either be a letter or digit, or must belong to the `VALID_NAME_SET`

snippet1.comprehensibility: This one is easy to understand: the logic is simple and the identifiers helped me understand what the method is doing.

snippet2.summary: it checks that the given URI is remote. If the URI is a file system path, does not has a scheme, of the scheme is a file or jar, it is not remote; otherwise; it is remote
snippet2.comprehensibility:
easy to understand: easy logic, identifiers helped here too.
it is harder to understand that snippet 1: I am not sure how isFilesystemPath works and what a scheme of a URI is

snippet3.summary: It checks that the provided machine type is defined: it is not unknown (as defined by the IMAGE_FILE_MACHINE_UNKNOWN) and is one of the short constants declared in the CoffMachineType class
snippet3.comprehensibility:
easy to understand here as well: while it uses reflection, it is easy to understand that it is trying to compare the provided input with the constants declared in CoffMachineType
I am unsure what a synthetic field is.
If a person is unfamiliar with Java constants, this snippet might be harder to understand.

snippet4.summary: This snippet is trying to find searchStr within str starting from startPos, ignoring the case. I suppose it returns the index in str where searchStr matches, guarding against going out of bounds in str.
snippet4.comprehensibility: This one was harder to understand: I don't really know what regionMatches does (I am supposing it matches strings within a range of indices). The logic here is more complex. The identifiers help a lot here.

snippet5.summary: Deletes recursively a path, which represents a file or a directory. It uses a visitor to delete files and directories within the path. I assume the path itself gets deleted as well. It returns true if the path was successfully deleted.
snippet5.comprehensibility: Easy to understand method. The visitor pattern is familiar to me and the methods it implements make it clear that the method deletes the path recursively. The identifiers also helped me grasp what the method is doing.

snippet6.summary: It seems the method is trying to determine the length of the provided sequence if encoded in UTF8.
snippet6.comprehensibility: Hard to understand method. I am not familiar with UTF8 vs UTF16 enconding. Unclear what encodedLengthGeneral does. It seems it is possible that utf8Length < utf16Length: lines 14 and 16 might sum a negative integer. I don't really understand the bit shifts here: it is been a while since I used them. However, I could probably figure it out if I did some google searching, to refresh memory and understand how certain statements work.

snippet7.summary: Finds the smallest positive root of a quadratic equation with constants a, b, and c.
snippet7.comprehensibility: Easy to understand method once I remembered the quadratic formula (I did a Google search). The logic is straightforward. At the beginning, I had no clue what the method was doing, but as I saw the operations within the method, it made me remember about quadratic equations and the formula to solve them. I need to Google this to be sure.

snippet8.summary: I don't know what the method is doing, but by the name of it, it may be computing the arctang of x and y (sine and cosine?).
snippet8.comprehensibility: Cryptic lines 4-7. I dont' remember arctang.

phase3.comparison:1-2:1|2:
both snippets implement simple logic and their overall intent is easy to understand -- the identifiers help quite a bit. 

S2 is slightly harder to understand because it is unclear why checking for file and jar is enough to determine that the URI is not remote. I guess my familiarity with the URI's scheme is not enough to fully understand the method.

This ordering is also consistent with the one from my colleagues.
phase3.comparison:2-3:2|7: Both implement simple logic as well, but S7 requires people to understand/remember the quadratic formula, hence it is slightly harder to understand than S2.
phase3.comparison:3-4:7|4: I think the logic of S4 is more complex, although I understand what it does -- it requires more nuanced understanding
phase3.comparison:4-5:4|5: S5 requires knowing the visitor pattern to fully understand what it does -- it can be obscure for people unfamiliar with the pattern. S4, on the other hand, doesn't implement any patterns: it is just regular code.
phase3.comparison:5-6:5|3: I guess reflection is harder to understand than the visitor pattern, as my colleagues point out. To me, both are pretty straightforward to understand, though, because I am familiar with these concepts and the identifiers make both pretty easy to understand.
phase3.comparison:6-7:3|6: not idea what the UFT8-2-UT16 entails, so for sure S6 is harder to understand than S3
phase3.comparison:7-8:6|8: atan2 is just very hard to understand because I don't remember how arctan's math works -- this is by far the hardest to understand for me.
